articles.title: Daily AI Briefing — Thursday, June 11, 2026 ET
articles.summary:
Daily AI Briefing — Thursday, June 11, 2026 ET

1. Today’s actual signal
- OpenAI Codex `0.139.0` shipped practical agent plumbing: standalone web search in Code mode, better MCP schema handling for `oneOf`/`allOf`, better `codex doctor`, and sandbox/proxy fixes. Source: https://github.com/openai/codex/releases — credible because it’s the official OpenAI Codex GitHub release page.
- GitHub Copilot is tightening the “agent work loop”: Copilot Chat can now see/search past agent sessions and pull agent logs, while Copilot CLI added experimental `/security-review` for local changes. Sources: https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-10-copilot-chat-now-sees-your-agent-sessions and https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-10-dedicated-security-review-command-now-available-in-copilot-cli — credible because both are GitHub Changelog posts.
- Google says Gemini CLI and Gemini Code Assist IDE extensions stop serving consumer/free requests on June 18, 2026, with migration to Antigravity CLI. Source: https://developers.googleblog.com/an-important-update-transitioning-gemini-cli-to-antigravity-cli/ — credible because it’s the Google Developers Blog; pricing/access risk: free Gemini CLI workflows may break next week.

2. Dustin relevance
- Hermes/agents: Codex’s MCP schema fixes and sandbox/proxy fixes are directly relevant to Hermes tool-heavy agent runs; worth testing before upgrading daily workflows. Source: https://github.com/openai/codex/releases
- Fanbase Weather: No strong signal today.
- Newsletter/Data Nerve: The useful framing is “agent products are becoming auditable work systems, not chat boxes” — GitHub’s agent logs/session search and CLI security review are concrete examples. Sources: https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-10-copilot-chat-now-sees-your-agent-sessions and https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-10-dedicated-security-review-command-now-available-in-copilot-cli
- App/product ideas: Lightweight “agent run memory + audit trail” dashboard for local agents is increasingly validated by GitHub’s session-search/log tooling and OpenAI’s Codex diagnostics work. Sources: https://github.blog/changelog/2026-06-10-copilot-chat-now-sees-your-agent-sessions and https://github.com/openai/codex/releases

3. Try / Save / Ignore
- Try today: In 20 minutes, run one scratch Hermes/Codex task that uses MCP/tools and compare behavior before/after Codex `0.139.0` release notes; do not change default config unless it clearly improves the run. Source: https://github.com/openai/codex/releases
- Save for later: Google Antigravity CLI migration docs, because free Gemini CLI access changes on June 18. Source: https://developers.googleblog.com/an-important-update-transitioning-gemini-cli-to-antigravity-cli/
- Ignore: “new frontier model” discourse unless it changes price, access, coding-agent reliability, or local/free tooling; today’s actionable changes are workflow plumbing, not benchmark claims.

4. Content angles
- “The next AI advantage is receipts: logs, session search, security review, and repeatable agent runs.”
- “Free AI tooling is not a strategy unless you track deprecations — Gemini CLI is the reminder.”

5. One next action
- Add a tiny local note/table for agent tools Dustin actually uses: tool, current version, last checked source link, and “breakage risk.” Start with Codex and Gemini/Antigravity; keep it to 10 minutes.

Verification: sources checked: OpenAI/Codex, Anthropic, xAI, Google AI/Developers, GitHub Copilot, Hacker News/Product Hunt/GitHub discovery; repetition check performed against available local state/log searches with no prior AI briefing outputs found beyond last-run state; no external side effects.
articles.whyItMatters: This is the vetted Daily AI Briefing output created for Dustin, replacing the old generic AI RSS/news-card section.
articles.sourceLabel: Hermes Daily AI Brief · Jun 11, 4:56 AM ET

articles.title: Kentucky Industrial Alliance says proposed Cave City data center could bring millions in tax revenue
articles.summary: WBKO is reporting on Kentucky Industrial Alliance says proposed Cave City data center could bring millions in tax revenue. In plain English, this is a large-load planning story: the important details are whether data centers are driving new power demand, who pays for the grid upgrades, and how regulators or lawmakers protect ordinary ratepayers. For Dustin, the useful read is whether data-center demand is starting to pressure utility forecasts, grid planning, rate design, or public communication around who pays for new infrastructure. This is included in the morning brief because it is dated, source-linked, and matched the scan filters for this section.
articles.emailSummary: A data-center story with possible implications for power demand, grid upgrades, and who pays for capacity.
articles.whyItMatters: Data-center load can reshape utility forecasts, infrastructure plans, rate design, reliability risk, and the analytics needed to separate real demand from speculation.
